info.title: Knowledge Service API
info.description:
# Overview

Knowledge Service is designed to be used by AI agents to retrieve semantically relevant information for answering user queries.
For the information owners it provides APIs to ingest documents, organize them into libraries, and manage access control.

## Getting Started

**PRIOR TO USING THIS API, YOU MUST AGREE TO OUR TERMS OF SERVICE**

The API is protected by OAuth2.0 and requires a valid TID access token in the `Authorization` header. Refer to [TID Authentication Guide](https://developer.trimble.com/docs/authentication) for details.

### Required TID Token Scopes

The Knowledge Service API requires the following TID token scopes:

- `openid` - Get user identity
- `kb` - Use Knowledge Base Service

## Capabilities Overview

- Organize documents into libraries
  - Control access to all documents in the library with access policies
  - Discover libraries you have access to by listing available libraries
  - Govern the metadata schema for documents within the library

- Document Management
  - Document level metadata
    - For now metadata schema is fixed and defined by the system namespace, later we might introduce additional configurable namespaces.
  - Coming: Document level access control on retrieval

- Retrieval and search capabilities
  - Search for documents across multiple libraries
  - Semantic (vector) search
  - Full-text (keyword) search
  - Hybrid search combining semantic and keyword search
  - Metadata based filtering to reduce the search space
  - Ranked retrieval of document chunks stored across multiple libraries

- Discovery and retrieval functionality is exposed as [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification) tools.

## Common API Concepts

This API uses HTTPS requests with JSON-formatted content in requests and responses.

### Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

This API features Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) implemented in compliance with the
[W3C spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/cors/) that allows cross-domain communication from the browser.
All responses have a wildcard same-origin which makes them completely public and accessible to everyone,
including any code on any site.

### Date Format

All dates in the API use UTC and are strings in the [ISO 8601 combined date and time representation
format](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Combined_date_and_time_representations)
Example: `2025-06-15T15:50:38.311456Z`.

### Eventual Read Consistency

The API responses are eventually consistent.
When you read data from the service, the response might not reflect the results of the most recently completed
write operation. The response might include some stale data due to caching
in place. If you repeat your read request after a short time, the response should return the latest data.

### Paginated Responses

All endpoints that return collection of resources follow the same schema for query parameters and
response structure to paginate the results.
Pagination allows to limit the size of the large responses and aimed to improve the responsiveness and overall
performance when fetching large result sets. The overall latency is improved
by parallelization of data processing and transfer.

Pagination is cursor-based and client-driven. The requests accept `skipToken` and `pageSize` query parameters.

If the `skipToken` query parameter is not specified, first page of the result set is returned.

The `pageSize` parameter informs the server on how many items client
wants to receive in a single page at most. Server may return less number of items than requested if requested response payload is too large or if some of the items are not accessible to the requester.
If `pageSize` parameter is not specified then server return maximum possible number of items by default.

The maximum page size is different for each endpoint and specified as part of the endpoint description.
It may also vary depending on the request parameters (e.g. `fields` query parameter).
If the `pageSize` parameter is greater than specified limit endpoint can support server will return error to help client avoid bug in pagination implementation.

The `links.next.href` property in the response contains the URL to the next page of results. If there are no more results, the `links.next.href` property is not present in the response.
Server may return response with `links.next.href` property even if the response contains no / lesser than requested(pageSize) items.
Server may return less items than `pageSize` requested (or even zero). It could happen e.g. if resources requested are too large to fit into a single response
or if some (or all `pageSize` requested) resources are not accessible to the requester. In this case requester is expected to call `links.next.href` to fetch the rest of the resources, if `links.next.href` is present in the response.
In extreme cases response could be empty with the `links.next.href` present, it means that all resources on the current page is not accessible to the requester.

#### Common Query Parameters

In addition to query parameters used for paging responses (like `pageSize`, `skipToken`) there are several query
parameters that are commonly used across all APIs.

- `state` - this query parameter is used to work with resources marked as "soft-deleted". The default APIs
(except APIs that access specific versions) behavior is to respond with 404 error when resource marked as deleted
is requested (or don't return deleted resource as part of the listing/search API). If this parameter is specified (`state=deleted`)
then deletion marker will be ignored and all resources are returned.
- the `fields` query parameter is used to specify the fields (properties) to be included in the response. If not provided, minimal number of fields will be included as default. The `fields` parameter is an array of field names passed as a comma separated array values, e.g. `fields=description,stats`. The list of supported fields is defined for each resource type.
- the `sortBy` query parameter is used to specify the order of the items in the response. If not provided, the default order is applied (typically creation timestamp). The `sortBy` parameter is a string that can be one of the predefined values, e.g. `+createdAt`, `-createdAt`. The `-` and `+` prefixes are used to specify the order direction (ascending or descending). Default order is ascending if no prefix is specified.
- the `search` query parameter is used to filter the items in the response. If specified, only items that match the search criteria are returned. The search criteria is a substring that is matched against the specific fields of the resource.

- all identifiers are represented as strings, client should not make assumptions about the format of the identifiers, they are opaque to the client
- all APIs return `x-request-id` header that contains a unique identifier for the request. This identifier can be used to track the request in the logs and for debugging purposes.

## Data Model

Information is organized into `libraries`, which are top-level containers for information, representing a knowledge base.
Libraries allow to group related documents, govern metadata schema and manage access control.

Each library can contain multiple `documents`, which are the primary units of knowledge. Documents typically point to the original source of information, such as a PDF file, web page, or other content. Original file is not stored in the knowledge service, but rather a reference to it is stored in the document metadata.

For now all libraries are associated with the the default system namespace that dictates the metadata schema for all documents in the library.

Documents divided to `chunks`, which are smaller pieces of information that can be semantically searched and used as a grounding information in the LLM conversation context window.

Service supports retrieval of chunks using semantic search and keyword search across multiple libraries.

### Library quota

Each library has a quota that limits the maximum total size of document content (e.g. 500MB by default). The quota is set when creating a library via the optional `quotas` field in the create-library request. If `quotas` is omitted, the default maximum content size is used. The quota cannot be changed via the API; the quota can only be changed by a request to the Agentic AI team.

## Search and Filtering

Service supports full-text fuzzy search for libraries and semantic, full-text, and hybrid search for document chunks across multiple libraries.

***Important*** When searching for library resources, translation tables are NOT searched, only the default `name` and `description` values. Same with sorting and filtering, translated values are NOT taken into account.

### Filtering

Filters allow to narrow down results of the listing and search operations. Common filters are supported across several endpoints.
The filter query follows the [FIQL (Feed Item Query Language)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-nottingham-atompub-fiql-00) syntax.

#### Query operators

The following table lists the valid query operators:

| Operator    | Alias | Usage                                                                                               |
| ----------- | ----- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `==`        |       | Equals. Matches if field value equals the specified value.                                          |
| `!=`        |       | Not equals. Matches if field value does not equal the specified value.                              |
| `=gt=`      | `>`   | Greater than. Matches if field value is greater than the specified value.                           |
| `=ge=`      | `>=`  | Greater than or equal. Matches if field value is greater than or equal to the specified value.      |
| `=lt=`      | `<`   | Less than. Matches if field value is less than the specified value.                                 |
| `=le=`      | `<=`  | Less than or equal. Matches if field value is less than or equal to the specified value.            |
| `=in=`      |       | In list. Matches if field value is in the specified list: `field=in=(val1,val2,val3)`.              |
| `=out=`     |       | Not in list. Matches if field value is NOT in the specified list: `field=out=(val1,val2)`.          |
| `;` (AND)   |       | Logical AND. Combines multiple conditions where all must match: `field1==val1;field2==val2`.        |
| `,` (OR)    |       | Logical OR. Combines multiple conditions where at least one must match: `field1==val1,field2==val2`.|

**Datetime format:** Datetime values must be in ISO 8601 format with a time component. Supported formats: `2024-01-01T00:00:00Z`, `2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z`, `2024-01-01T00:00:00+00:00`. Date-only values (e.g., `2024-01-01`) are not supported. If no timezone is specified, UTC is assumed.

#### Query terms (selectors)

##### Chunk search selectors (`POST /search`)

The following table lists all valid filter fields for the chunk search endpoint. These fields filter on document-level metadata attributes.

| Selector            | Type     | Valid operators                                        | Usage                                                                        |
| ------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `tags`              | array    | `=in=`, `=out=`                                        | Filter by document metadata tags.                                            |
| `source.contentType`| string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by document type, e.g. `application/pdf`.                             |
| `source.size`       | numeric  | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document size in bytes.                                     |
| `source.createdAt`  | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document creation timestamp.                                |
| `source.updatedAt`  | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document last update timestamp.                             |
| `source.createdBy`  | string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by source document creator.                                           |
| `source.updatedBy`  | string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by source document last updater.                                      |

##### Document listing selectors (`GET /libraries/{libraryId}/documents`)

The following table lists all valid filter fields for the document listing endpoint. These fields filter on document-level metadata attributes.

| Selector            | Type     | Valid operators                                        | Usage                                                                        |
| ------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `tags`              | array    | `=in=`, `=out=`                                        | Filter by document metadata tags.                                            |
| `source.contentType`| string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by document type, e.g. `application/pdf`.                             |
| `source.size`       | numeric  | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document size in bytes.                                     |
| `source.createdAt`  | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document creation timestamp.                                |
| `source.updatedAt`  | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`             | Filter by source document last update timestamp.                             |
| `source.createdBy`  | string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by source document creator.                                           |
| `source.updatedBy`  | string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by source document last updater.                                      |
| `source.title`      | string   | `==`, `!=`                                             | Filter by source document title                                              |

**Note on tag filtering with `=in=` and `=out=`:**

- Comma-separated values use OR logic: `tags=in=(product:tekla-structures,lang:en)` matches documents with **any** of the listed tags.
- Multiple `=in=` clauses joined with `;` use AND logic: `tags=in=(product:tekla);tags=in=(lang:en,lang:fi)` matches documents with tag `product:tekla` **and** any of `lang:en` or `lang:fi`.
- `=out=` excludes documents matching any of the listed tags: `tags=out=(version:2025)` excludes documents with tag `version:2025`.

##### Library selectors (`GET /libraries`, `GET /search-v2`)

The following table lists all valid filter fields for library listing and library search endpoints.

| Selector         | Type    | Valid operators | Usage                                                                                        |
| ---------------- | ------- | --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `effectiveRoles` | special | `=in=`          | Context-sensitive filter. Only supports value `admin`. Returns libraries where the current user is an admin. |

#### Query string examples

**Tag filtering - documents with any of the specified tags (OR):**

```
filter=tags=in=(product:tekla-structures,version:2025,lang:en)
```

**Tag filtering - documents with all specified tags (AND via semicolon):**

```
filter=tags=in=(product:tekla-structures);tags=in=(version:2025);tags=in=(lang:en)
```

**Tag filtering - include and exclude tags:**

```
filter=tags=in=(product:viewpoint,lang:en);tags=out=(version:2025)
```

**Tag filtering - one required tag and any of several language tags:**

```
filter=tags=in=(product:trimble-connect);tags=in=(lang:fi,lang:en,lang:de)
```

**Source attribute - filter by content type:**

```
filter=source.contentType==application/pdf
```

**Source attribute - filter by document size:**

```
filter=source.size=gt=10000
```

**Source attribute - filter by creation date:**

```
filter=source.createdAt=gt=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Source attribute - datetime range:**

```
filter=source.createdAt=ge=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z;source.createdAt=lt=2024-07-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Source attribute - filter by creator:**

```
filter=source.createdBy==users:content-author
```

**Combined tag and source attribute filtering:**

```
filter=tags=in=(product:tekla-structures);source.size=gt=5000
```

```
filter=source.contentType==application/pdf;source.createdAt=gt=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Library filtering - find libraries where current user is admin:**

```
filter=effectiveRoles=in=(admin)
```

## Soft and Hard Delete, Data Retention

Soft delete marks the resource as deleted without removing it from the database. This allows for recovery if needed. Hard delete removes the resource permanently.

Data retention policies define how long data is kept before being deleted. This is important for compliance and legal reasons.

### Deleting Libraries

Libraries support three states:

1. **Soft Delete (deleted state)**: When a library is deleted by an administrator without the `permanent=true` parameter, it does not erase the data but marks the library as deleted and blocks further interactions. The library can be recovered from the soft delete state by executing any mutation operation on it with the `state=deleted` query parameter.

2. **Delete Pending (delete_pending state)**: When a library is deleted with `permanent=true`, it enters the delete_pending state. In this state:
   - The library cannot be accessed or recovered
   - A background cleanup job will permanently remove the library and all associated documents and chunks from the database

3. **Active State**: Normal operational state where the library can be used.

To ignore the deletion status of a resource, API endpoints accept the `state` query parameter (only for Admin role). Available values are:

- `state=active` - only active libraries (default)
- `state=deleted` - only soft-deleted libraries

If the state parameter is not passed and the resource is soft deleted, APIs will return a 404 error.

Note: The identifier of a soft deleted resource stays reserved and is not available to be used by other resources. Once permanently deleted (removed by the background job), the identifier becomes available for reuse.

To delete a library permanently, use the delete endpoint with the `permanent=true` query parameter. This marks the library for deletion by the background cleanup job.

### Deleting Documents

User (with appropriate permissions) can delete documents. In this case the document is immediately deleted (with all its chunks) and not recoverable.

### Data Retention

Soft deleted libraries are automatically purged after a certain period of time (30 days).
Otherwise the data is retained indefinitely until explicitly deleted.

### Access Control Model

The access control is based on roles. The `LibraryCreator`, `Admin` and `User`.

- `LibraryCreator` is a system-wide role that gives a privilege to create new libraries.
Typically this role is assigned to users based on the licenses they have. There are several license providers supported (EMS integration is coming soon).
All Trimble employees are automatically assigned a license that gives this role (well know license id with full access for Trimble employees is `00581fef-acde-4f9a-8a25-e05f529e8cb5`).

- `Admin` is a role in the scope of a specific "library".
  Actors with this role can access the libraries, perform chunk retrieval on libraries, delete libraries, and manage libraries's permissions. The `Admin` can also do everything that `User` can do in the resource scope.

- `User` is a role in the scope of a specific "library".
  Actors with `User` role can read documents from libraries, and perform chunk retrieval on libraries.

Actors are assigned roles in the scope of specific resources using claims that refer to various information associated with the actor.

Supported claims are:

- `users:<id>` - - TID user identity, present if actor identity type is `user` (source is TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `applications:<id>` - TID application identity, present if actor identity type is `application` (source is TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `accounts:<id>` - user's IAM account identifier (source is TID JWT `account_id` claim)
- `trimble-employee:true|false` - indicates whether actor belongs to Trimble organization (TID JWT `amr` claim and IAM is a source of this information)
- `trimble-automation:true|false` - indicates whether actor is Trimble test automation user ((TID JWT `amr` claim is a source of this information))
- `licenses-aaip:<id>` - the identifier of license provided by AAIP the actor has

A special `*` value can be used with `users:`, `applications:` and `licenses-aaip:` claim types to refer to any authenticated user, application or presence of a AAIP license.

For example

- `users:123456789` refers to the user with TID id `123456789`.
- `applications:*` refers to any authenticated application.
- `accounts:987` refers to any user from account `987`.
- `trimble-employee:true` claim present for all Trimble employees.
- `trimble-automation:true` claim present for all Trimble automation users.

## Error Handling

### Handling Errors and Retry Logic

Common HTTP Response Status Codes are used.
The response body for all error responses are in the same format and include the error code and message.

When you send requests to and get responses from the Service API, you might encounter two types of API errors

- **Client errors** - Client errors are indicated by a 4xx HTTP response code. Client errors indicate
that the service found a problem with the client request. This can include such things as an authentication
failure or missing required parameters. Fix the issue in the client application before submitting the request again.
- **Server errors** - Server errors are indicated by a 5xx HTTP response code and need to be resolved by Trimble.
You may resubmit/retry the request until it succeeds.

For each API error, the API Gateway returns the following values

- A status code, for example, `400`
- An error type, for example, `tag:ValidationException`
- An error message, for example, `Supplied AttributeValue is empty, must contain exactly one of the
supported data types`

### Error Response Body Schema

All error responses follow the same structure for the response body and are conformant to [RFC 9457](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9457.html) specification.
The error types use `tag:` URI schema and cannot be dereferenced.

| Error type                                | HTTP Status Code | Error description |
| ----------------------------------------- | --- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `tag:BadGateway`                          | 503 | Error communicating with downstream service.|
| `tag:ConflictingHeaders`                  | 400 | Only one of If-Match or If-None-Match header is allowed in a request.|
| `tag:Conflict`                            | 409 | Conflict is detected on concurrent resource mutation.|
| `tag:DatabaseOperationFailed`             | 500 | Database operation failed.|
| `tag:InternalServerError`                 | 500 | The internal server error, please try again later.|
| `tag:InvalidAcl`                          | 400 | The ACL cannot be empty on space level.|
| `tag:InvalidIfMatchHeader`                | 400 | Invalid If-Match Header.|
| `tag:InvalidFilterExpression`             | 400 | Filter parameter is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidLicense`                      | 403 | The specified license is not available for this user.|
| `tag:InvalidName`                         | 400 | The resource name is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidPageSize`                     | 400 | The request contains invalid page size.|
| `tag:InvalidParentParam`                  | 400 | The parent param is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidPayload`                      | 400 | One of id or name is mandatory. In case of a new file upload name is mandatory.|
| `tag:InvalidQueryParam`                   | 400 | Invalid query param: X.|
| `tag:InvalidRequest`                      | 400 | Invalid request.|
| `tag:InvalidSkipToken`                    | 400 | The request contains invalid skip token.|
| `tag:InvalidSortParameter`                | 400 | The given sort parameter is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidStateParam`                   | 400 | The state param is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidUpload`                       | 400 | The file status is not UPLOADABLE.|
| `tag:InvalidUrl`                          | 404 | The url is not valid.|
| `tag:InvalidVersion`                      | 412 | Specified version is not the latest version.|
| `tag:InvalidParam`                        | 400 | Please provide a valid version.|
| `tag:LibraryQuotaExceeded`                | 403 | The operation would exceed the library quota.|
| `tag:HttpError`                           | xxx | Generic HTTP error.|
| `tag:MethodNotAllowed`                    | 405 | Method is not allowed on this resource.|
| `tag:MissingAccountId`                    | 403 | The account_id claim is missing from the user's JWT token.|
| `tag:MissingLicense`                      | 403 | License not found. Agent must be associated with a license on creation.|
| `tag:NotAcceptableMediaType`              | 406 | The requested media type is not supported.|
| `tag:PageSizeExceeded`                    | 400 | The pageSize parameter exceeds the maximum supported page size (N).|
| `tag:PermissionDenied`                    | 403 | Permission denied for the requested resource.|
| `tag:ResourceNotFound`                    | 404 | The file or folder not found.|
| `tag:StaleObject`                         | 400 | Cannot update as request contains stale data.|
| `tag:ServiceUnavailable`                  | 500 | Service not available temporarily.|
| `tag:TokenExchangeError`                  | 400 | Failed to exchange TID token to communicate with the downstream services.|
| `tag:UnAuthorized`                        | 401 | The request is not authorized.|
| `tag:UnprocessableContent`                | 422 | The request content is invalid.|

### Error Status Codes (Client and Server Errors)

HTTP status codes indicate whether an operation is successful or not.
A response code of 2xx indicates that the operation was successful. Other error codes indicate either a
client error (4xx) or a server error (5xx).
The error table lists the errors returned by the service. Some errors are resolved if you retry the same request.
As a general rule, the timeout errors, network errors, throttling errors, and 5xx errors may be retried.
The 401 error code usually indicates expired credentials and should be retried after refreshing the access token.
The error table indicates which errors are likely to be resolved with successive retries.

If the value of the Retry column is

- **Yes** - Submit the same request again.
- **Yes if idempotent** - Submit the same request again, but only if the given method has been
implemented with idempotence.
- **No** - Fix the problem on the client-side before submitting a new request.

For more information about retrying requests, see Error Retries and Exponential Backoff.

| HTTP Status Code | Error type class                      | Retry                                             |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| 400              |  Bad Request Exception                | No                                                |
| 401              |  Unauthorized Exception               | No, could be retried after acquiring a new token  |
| 403              |  Access Denied Exception              | No                                                |
| 404              |  Not Found Exception                  | No                                                |
| 406              |  Not Acceptable Exception             | No                                                |
| 409              |  Conflict Exception                   | No                                                |
| 429              |  Too Many Requests Exception          | Yes                                               |
| 500              |  Internal Service Failure Exception   | Yes                                               |
| 502              |  Bad Gateway Exception                | Yes if idempotent                                 |
| 503              |  Service Unavailable Exception        | Yes                                               |
| 504              |  Endpoint Request Timed-out Exception | Yes if idempotent                                 |

### Catching Errors

For your application to run smoothly, you need to build in logic to catch and respond to errors.
One typical approach is to implement your request within a try block or if-then statement.
The client-side SDKs typically perform retries and error checking out of the box.

The error code and description are always send in the request response body in addition to HTTP status code.
To troubleshoot the problem please capture the request identifier using the `x-request-id`, `X-Azure-Ref`, `x-amzn-RequestId` HTTP response headers. Inform the value received in these headers to support team.

### Error Retries and Exponential Backoff

Numerous components on a network, such as DNS servers, switches, load balancers,
and others can generate errors anywhere in the life of a given request.

The usual technique for dealing with these error responses in a networked environment
is to implement retries in the client application.
This technique increases the reliability of the application and reduces operational costs for the developer.

If you're not using the official SDKs, you should retry original requests that receive server errors (5xx).
However, client errors (4xx, other than a TooManyRequestsException) indicate you need to
revise the request itself to correct the problem before trying again.

In addition to simple retries, it is recommended to use an exponential backoff algorithm for better flow control.
The idea behind exponential backoff is to use progressively longer waits between retries
for consecutive error responses.
For example, you might let one second elapse
before the first retry, four seconds before the second retry, 16 seconds before the third retry, and so on.
However, if the request has not succeeded after a minute, the problem might be a hard limit and not the
request rate.
For example, you may have reached the maximum number of pipelines allowed. Set the maximum
number of retries to stop around one minute.

## Payload compression

The service accepts `gzip` and `br` compressed request payloads. The `Content-Encoding` header must be sent in the request
to decode the request payload properly.

The service supports the following content encodings for responses:

- `gzip`
- `br`
- `identity`

Only responses larger than 1KB might be encoded. Small responses are sent without encoding.

The encoding (compression) method can be negotiated with the `Accept-Encoding` header.
The following `Accept-Encoding` header formats, according to the [RFC 7231](<https://tools.ietf.org/html/>
rfc7231#section-5.3.4) specification, are supported:

- `Accept-Encoding:br,gzip`
- `Accept-Encoding:` - If the Accept-Encoding header is present and empty, only the unencoded content is acceptable.
- `Accept-Encoding:*` - This is the default value if the header is not present. This directive does not suggest that any algorithm is supported but indicates that no preference is expressed. Service will decide on the best compression method.
- `Accept-Encoding:br;q=0.5,gzip=1.0`
- `Accept-Encoding:gzip;q=1.0,identity;q=0.5,*;q=0`
If the header is not set, the default value is `*` as defined in RFC 7231. In such cases, the payload is compressed with best supported compression method.

## MCP Server

Selected APIs are are exposed as [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification) tools in addition to traditional REST endpoints.

The MCP server endpoint is <baseUrl>/v1/mcp. The protocol is Streamable HTTP.

Following tools are supported:

- `ListLibraries`
- `ListTags`
- `ListDocuments`
- `GetDocument`
- `ListChunks`
- `Search`
- `ListNeighborChunks`

You can connect to the MCP server with standard tools like [MCP inspector](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/legacy/tools/inspector) or [Cursor](https://docs.cursor.com/en/context/mcp).

MCP server supports protected resource metadata protocol [RFC9728](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9728/) to enable seamless authentication.

Example Cursor IDE configuration:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "Trimble Knowledge Base": {
      "url": "https://kb.stage.trimble-ai.com/v1/mcp",
    }
  }
}
```

## Localization

The Knowledge Service API supports localization of libraries through the `translations` field in the library configuration and the `Accept-Language` HTTP header.

### Translation Table

Library configurations can include a `translations` field containing a dictionary of language-specific translations for the following properties:

- `name` - The library name
- `description` - The library description

The translation table is a dictionary where keys are language tags (e.g., `en-US`, `fi`, `de-DE`) and values are objects containing any combination of the localizable properties listed above.

**Example translation table:**

```json
{
  "translations": {
    "fi": {
      "name": "Suomenkielinen kirjasto",
      "description": "Kirjasto suomenkielisille sisällöille"
    },
    "de-DE": {
      "name": "Deutschsprachige Bibliothek",
      "description": "Bibliothek mit deutschsprachigen Inhalten"
    }
  }
}
```

### Accept-Language Header

The `GET /libraries` and `GET /libraries/{libraryId}` endpoints respect the `Accept-Language` HTTP header for content negotiation:

- If the `Accept-Language` header is **not present**, the API returns the default (non-translated) values for all properties
- If the `Accept-Language` header **is present**, the API attempts to find a matching language tag in the library's translation table:
  - Language tags are matched using **case-insensitive exact string comparison**
  - The API follows the language preferences specified in the `Accept-Language` header in order of quality values (as defined in [RFC 7231](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-5.3.5))
  - No additional fallback rules are implemented (e.g., `en-US` will not automatically fall back to `en`)
  - Only properties present in the matched translation entry are replaced; other properties retain their default values

**Example Accept-Language headers:**

- `Accept-Language: fi` - Request Finnish translations
- `Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9,fi;q=0.8` - Prefer US English, then English, then Finnish
- `Accept-Language: de-DE` - Request German (Germany) translations

### Content-Language Response Header

When a matching translation is found and applied, the API includes a `Content-Language` response header containing the matched language tag from the translation table.

- If no `Accept-Language` header is present: no `Content-Language` header is returned
- If `Accept-Language` is present but no matching translation exists: no `Content-Language` header is returned
- If a matching translation is found: `Content-Language` header contains the matched language tag (e.g., `Content-Language: fi`)

### Configuration Limits

- Maximum number of language entries in the translation table: 30
- Each translated property is subject to the same length constraints as the default property

## Limits

The service has the following limits:

- Maximum library name length - 64
- Maximum library description length - 1024
- Maximum number of libraries for cross-library retrieval - 10
- Maximum number of languages in translation table - 30

delete.description:
Deletes a knowledge base library. By default the operation performs a soft delete. A soft delete marks the library as deleted and blocks further interactions with it. Library in soft-delete state can be restored by performing a mutation operation on it with the `state=deleted` query parameter.

If the `permanent` query parameter is set to `true`, the library is marked for permanent deletion (delete_pending state). The library and all its associated documents and chunks will be permanently removed by a background cleanup job. Once in delete_pending state, the library cannot be accessed or recovered.

**Note:** Permanent deletion is asynchronous. The library enters delete_pending state immediately, but the actual removal from the database is handled by a background job.

post.description:
Creates a new document in the library.

This request immediately creates a first content generation for the document where chunks can be uploaded to (the generation is not active yet).This request includes information for both document and generation creation.The metadata in the request (`viewers`, `source`) is associated with the future generation as well as applied to the document immediately.

Operation is allowed only in active libraries.

Client might provide a document identifier in the creation request. This is useful for tracking the correlation with the original document source, e.g. the document identifier in the source system can be used directly.
The document identifier must be unique within the library, but might be not unique across libraries.
If the document identifier is not provided, the service generates a new unique identifier for the document.

After the document is created, it does not have any searchable content yet. The chunks must be uploaded to generation and generation must be published to make it searchable.

The chunks upload is a 3 phase process:
1. Create a generation for the document using the `CreateGeneration` API. This step can be skipped right after the document creation as the very first generation is created automatically.
If there are any existing staging generations of chunks that were not published yet, they will be deleted.
2. Upload the document chunks to the staging generation using the `UploadChunk` API. It supports bulk upload of up to 100 chunks in a single request. Chunk upload can be parallelized for better throughput.
3. Publish the generation using the `PublishGeneration` API. At this point if there were any previous published generations of chunks, they will be deleted.

post.description:
Promotes the "staging" generation of chunks to the "published" generation. Client must present a valid identifier of the current staging generation.

Operation atomically replaces the current published generation with the staging generation. In case of any errors the published generation remains unchanged.

In case of success, all chunks from the previous published generation are removed and the new generation becomes the new published generation, with metadata of the staging generation applied to the document.

After this operation, the document does not have any active staging generation. To change the document content, the `CreateGeneration` operation must be used to create a new staging generation.

This operation might take some time to complete, depending on the number of chunks.

post.description:
Uploads a batch of one or more chunks to a document generation. Chunks are always uploaded to the staging generation and not visible for retrieval until that generation is published.Only the actor who has created current staging generation can upload chunks to it.

Aim to maximize number of chunks per request as bulk upload is more efficient than creating chunks individually.

Chunks are immutable after upload. Obsolete chunks are deleted automatically when new generation is published.

Error handling strategy:
1. Fail fast the whole request in case of easy to check errors such as document not found, generation mismatch, request format not valid, etc.
2. After initial validation and data preparations, chunks are inserted to the database in bulk in parallel. This can result potentially in partial upload of chunks. Response has information about chunks that were successfully uploaded and chunks that failed to upload with detailed error information.

get.description:
List chunks of the specific document. 

The operation supports various filtering options including finding surrounding chunks for a given chunk id or fetching chunks by parent chunk id. If the chunk or parent chunk id is given as a filter, the API returns only chunks from the same generation as a given chunk, which might be not the current published generation. When listing chunks for the document without giving a chunk id, only chunks for the currently published generation are returned. 

Results are ordered by chunk index (from smallest index to largest). 

Operation is allowed only in active libraries.

post.description:
This is an API that expose the primary capability of the Knowledge Service - search and retrieval.

It allows users to find specific chunks of information based on their queries.

The API makes the best effort to search in the chunks that belong to the published generations of the document content. But the indexing of the chunks from recently published generation and removing the old chunks from the index might take time so brief inconsistencies should be expected.

The API does NOT support pagination, it will return max top 50 results that match the query.
Results are sorted by the score, most relevant first.
For the `semantic` and `hybrid` search types, the score is a cosine similarity value in the [0, 1] range.
For the `fulltext` search type, the underlying keyword relevance is normalized within the returned result set to the [0, 1] range:
- 1.0 = highest scoring chunk for that specific query
- Scores are only comparable within the same query execution, not across different queries
- Chunks with identical raw scores receive the same normalized score
- If only one result is returned, its score is 1.0

Flexible search capabilities are provided, allowing users to choose the search type: `fulltext`, `semantic` or `hybrid` as well as limit the search to specific libraries and metadata based condition.

- For the `fulltext` search, the query supports a subset of the Apache Lucene query syntax similar to the [Azure AI search simple query syntax](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/query-simple-syntax), allowing users to perform complex queries with logical operators, wildcards, and field-specific searches.
- For the `semantic` search, the query is a natural language text that is processed by the service to find relevant chunks based on their semantic meaning.
- The `hybrid` search combines semantic search with the [BM25](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Okapi_BM25) keyword search based ranking algorithm, allowing users to leverage the strengths of both approaches. This is a default search type and recommended for most use cases.

Examples of query syntax:
- `NY +view` - simple query with AND operator
- `restaurant -\"air conditioning\"` - negation and phrase search (phrases are enclosed in double quotes)

The `filter` parameter enables limiting the results based on document metadata attributes such as `tags`, `source.contentType`, `source.size`, `source.createdAt`, `source.updatedAt`, `source.createdBy`, and `source.updatedBy`. Refer to the `Search and Filtering` section of the API documentation for supported operators, selectors, and examples.
